coredata.dc:title: Unveiling real-world aging mechanisms of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yap, Jun Wei
Lee, Michael
El Idrissi, Faissal
Ramesh, Prashanth
Yoon, Dal Young
Kang, Chun Yong
Canova, Marcello
Cho, Hanna
Kim, Jung-Hyun
coredata.dc:description: This work focuses on understanding the degradation mechanisms of lithium-ion batteries in relation to driving distances of electric vehicles (EVs). Comprehensive electrochemical analysis successfully identified and quantified major degradation mechanisms of EV battery cells including active material loss, Li loss, and cell impedance. Cathode performance was the major limiting factor of the overall cell capacity given the N/P ratio exceeding 1.0. The impact of Li loss and active material loss mechanisms on performance degradation with total driven distance from 0 to 250 k km was visualized by simple, customized electrochemical methods. The Li loss predominantly governed the initial performance degradation of EV cells, while after 150 k km driving distance (i.e,. mileage), active material loss started to dominate the degradation. The degradation mechanism of cell impedance was analyzed using symmetric cell through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and distribution of relaxation times (DRT) techniques. With increasing mileage, the cathode suffered from charge transfer resistance, while the anode suffered from the contact impedance. This work demonstrates effective experimental strategies to reveal Li-ion battery degradation mechanisms in EVs, which can contribute to the continued development of future EV technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electric vehicles (EV) lithium-ion batteries
Post-mortem analysis
Degradation mechanisms
Electrochemical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of cycle-aged graphite anodes
Analysis of overall degradation mechanisms in EV cells

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RCID testing result of a representative fresh (0 k km) full-cell in this work. (a) Voltage profiles, (b) an extended view of pulse power test, and (c) current profiles of a full-cells recorded during a series of charge and discharge pulses performed at different SOCs.
(a) Residual capacity of cells measured at C/20-rate at 25 °C. (b) Resistance of cells with different mileage (i.e,. driving distance) at various state-of-charge (SOC) at 25 °C.
The impact of cell aging on differential capacity (dQ/dV) profiles of fresh, 100 k km, 200 k km-aged full-cells.

(a) Variation of residual capacities of graphite anodes depending on EV mileage (i.e., driving distance). The initial discharge capacities were recorded from CR2032 coin-type half-cells at 25 °C at C/20 between 1 V and 0.005 V. Variations of the residual capacities in half-cells using cycle-aged graphite anodes recovered from EV cells having (b) 100 k km and (c) 250 k km mileage.

(a) Voltage profile of cathodes recovered from EV cells having mileages (i.e., driving distances) of 0 km (fresh), 50 k km, 100 k km, 150 k km and 250 k km. (b) The amount of capacity loss (Ah) from aged cathodes due to Li-ion loss failure mechanism. These values were determined via Step 1, initial discharge process at 25 °C. (c) The residual, reversible capacity of aged cathodes determined via Step 2 (i.e., second) discharge process at 25 °C with C/20 between 2.5 V and 4.5 V.

(a) Bar chart plotting the contribution of Li-loss and active material loss (Act. Mat. Loss) to the total capacity loss depending on EV mileage (i.e., driving distance). (b) Mechanisms of capacity degradation in EV batteries: the loss mechanisms identified in this work agrees well with the actual full-cell capacity shown in Fig. 2a.

Nyquist plots and corresponding DRT profiles of (a, b) cathode symmetric cells and (c, d) anode symmetric cells with 50 k, 100 k, 250 k km mileages (i.e., driving distances). All data was recorded at 25 °C at 50 % SOC.
Procedure to prepare half-cells from EV full-cells.
Representative half-cell capacity of anode and cathode with 0 k km (fresh), 100 k km, and 250 k km mileages (i.e., driving distances), recorded at C/20-rate and 25 °C.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data was collected using single-layer pouch cells using aged cathode (area of 24 cm2) and fresh Li-metal anode at 25 °C.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data was collected using CR2032 coin half-cells using aged anode (area of 1.33 cm2) and fresh Li-metal anode at 25 °C.

head.ce:title: Unveiling real-world aging mechanisms of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Automotive Research, Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, The Ohio State University, Columbus, OH 43210, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Automotive Research, Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Automotive Research, Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, The Ohio State University, Columbus, OH 43210, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hyundai Motor Company, Hwaseong-si, Gyuonggi-do, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hyundai Motor Company, Hwaseong-si, Gyuonggi-do, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work focuses on understanding the degradation mechanisms of lithium-ion batteries in relation to driving distances of electric vehicles (EVs). Comprehensive electrochemical analysis successfully identified and quantified major degradation mechanisms of EV battery cells including active material loss, Li loss, and cell impedance. Cathode performance was the major limiting factor of the overall cell capacity given the N/P ratio exceeding 1.0. The impact of Li loss and active material loss mechanisms on performance degradation with total driven distance from 0 to 250 k km was visualized by simple, customized electrochemical methods. The Li loss predominantly governed the initial performance degradation of EV cells, while after 150 k km driving distance (i.e,. mileage), active material loss started to dominate the degradation. The degradation mechanism of cell impedance was analyzed using symmetric cell through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and distribution of relaxation times (DRT) techniques. With increasing mileage, the cathode suffered from charge transfer resistance, while the anode suffered from the contact impedance. This work demonstrates effective experimental strategies to reveal Li-ion battery degradation mechanisms in EVs, which can contribute to the continued development of future EV technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The rise in global commitment toward curbing carbon emissions has led to a substantial increase in the adoption of EVs [1,2]. This trend has resulted in much research into battery pack design, evaluation, and optimization. Many efforts are invested in exploring alternative materials, aiming to extended cycle life, speedup charging rates, and improved safety. Although the exploration into newer and better battery chemistry is important, there is an equally important need to comprehend the intricate process of battery aging in relation to usage duration [3]. Battery aging does not only significantly impact the longevity of battery electric vehicles, it also heavily affects other domains such as power electronics, power infrastructure, and broader battery systems.

Solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation is a prominent side reaction that occurs on the surface of graphite anode [4,5]. SEI layer is being produced during the cell's formation cycle, by decomposing electrolyte and result in deposition of insulating compounds. This process depletes active lithium ions and thus diminishes the achievable capacity [5]. Moving to the cathode side, a notable degradation especially relevant to high nickel content cathode is transition metal dissolution [6,7]. This side reaction accelerates SEI degradation and triggers parasitic reactions with the electrolyte, leading to impedance growth and capacity fade [5,8]. Additionally, undesirable morphological changes can occur in electrode due to stress induced by particle volume change during charge and discharge step [9,10]. These changes can lead to active material loss and promote more SEI growth. Understanding the intricacies of these side reactions is pivotal for designing effective strategies to mitigate their adverse effects. Therefore, further research is imperative to unravel the intricacies of these mechanisms, paving the way for enhanced Li-ion battery performance, longevity, and safety.

The investigation into battery aging comes in various forms, primarily manifesting through electrochemical analysis and spectroscopy evaluation. Each study is tailored toward uncovering specific facets of the cell degradation. Electrochemical analysis such as full-cell performance comparison, differential capacity (dQ/dV) analysis, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and distribution of relaxation times (DRT) technique serve as non-destructive means to unravel the temporal progression of aging [11–14]. Notably, these electrochemical characterization techniques can provide insights into the holistic cell behaviors in situ. Due to the non-destructive nature of these analysis methods, they are commonly employed to evaluate the state of health and degradation phenomena in operating energy storage stations. Dubarry et al. [15] demonstrated the effectiveness of these methods in assessing the station's state of health and predicting degradation trends for the coming years. Additionally, they can serve as in-situ tools to examine degradation mechanisms within individual cells [16].

While research into aging mechanisms is common, the majority of the research endeavors have been confined to laboratory-scale battery cells. This approach does provide invaluable insights into the intricacy of aging mechanisms under various abusive operating conditions. However, this confined scope lacks a comprehensive understanding of battery stability under real-world usage scenarios. In real-world, there will often be uncontrollable environmental variables that starkly contrast with the controlled laboratory conditions setup for testing. Compounding this challenge is the scarcity of access to actual battery cells sourced from EV applications. Our study aims to bridge this gap by adopting a detailed approach that involves electrochemical analysis to characterize the relationship between drive distance and aging severity. The underlying mechanisms of capacity loss and their growth as driving distance increases were presented.

In this work, aged battery cells were retrieved from battery packs of several EVs with different total driven distances (i.e., mileage) for analysis. After cycling, the cells were selected to represent the average performance characteristics of the battery pack. To minimize potential positional effects, all cells were chosen from mid-module positions. Li-ion battery cells harvested from EV battery packs were initially characterized to assess performance characteristics prior to cell tear down. Each cell was experimentally characterized under isothermal conditions. The cell was placed in a custom-built test fixture held in-between aluminum plates with pressure applied through compression springs. Each aluminum plate was cooled through the use of thermoelectric devices allowing for the cell temperature to be controlled within 0.5 °C. A tota1l of 5 cell samples were characterized by performing a capacity test with C/20 and C/3 discharge rate and a series of charge and discharge pulses performed at different state-of-charge (SOC) levels. The tests were performed at 25 °C.

All full-cells were fully discharged to 2.9 V using a constant current (CC) – constant voltage (CV) mode at C/20 rate before cell disassembly. The full-cells were disassembled in Ar-filled glovebox to avoid electrode damages caused by oxidation or moisture attack. All cycled electrodes obtained were rinsed with dimethyl carbonate (DMC) for 1 min each to remove residue electrolyte before sealing and storage in glovebox.

Cycled electrodes were made into half-cells to assess its electrochemical performance after aging. For cathode side, a 42 mm by 32 mm single layer pouch half-cells were made with thin Li metal sheets rolled by roller pin attached onto a copper foil as the counter electrode. For anode side, coin half-cells (CR2032) were made instead with the entire process completed within the glovebox. Scheme 1 describes the entire process. In both cell formats, liquid electrolyte consisting of 1 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate (EC) and ethylmethyl carbonate (EMC) in 1:1 volume ratio and a piece of polypropylene (Celgard 2500) separator were used.

Cells were placed in a 25 °C environmental chamber and tested using a battery cycler (Arbin LBT system). All half-cells were cycled at C/20 rate to avoid IR drop and capture the full capacity of the aged electrodes [4,17]. The cathodes were operating from 4.3 V to 3 V, while anodes were operating from 1 V to 0.005 V. All testing were completed with a battery cycling station Arbin LBT System) in an environmental chamber (at 25 °C). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Fisher Arpero) imaged the surface morphology of the aged electrodes recovered from the EV cells.

We first analyzed the power capabilities of the full-cells using RCID, a custom test profile similar to the standardized hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC) test. The voltage and current profile of the RCID test is shown in Fig. 1 . The test consists of a series of charge and discharge pulses performed at different SOCs. At each specified SOC, charge and discharge pulses are performed at different C-rates. Utilizing the voltage drop measured during a pulse discharge, the internal resistance of the cell can be estimated.

The results from the RCID tests performed on the fresh and aged full-cells are shown in Fig. 2 . The capacity measured from the C/3 discharge is shown in Fig. 2a, represented in terms of capacity (in Ah) against the driven distance (in thousand km) corresponding to the battery pack from which the sample was harvested. The 0 km corresponds to a fresh cell with a capacity close to the nominal value. It is important to note that Fig. 2a shows a non-linear decline in cell capacity with respect to vehicle distance. The degradation of the cell is most severe in the first 100 k km distance (∼10 % decrease in capacity), while the capacity decline is minimal going from 150 k km to 250 k km distance. This could be a combination of varying drive history or the performance tapering off due to stabilization of degradation such as SEI and active material loss [18–20]. Despite the varied driving histories and operational conditions of the EVs from which the batteries were recovered, the aged cells exhibited a clear trend of capacity fade with increasing driven distance.

Fig. 2b compares the DC resistance of the cells at different SOCs. The resistance is calculated by measuring the voltage drop throughout a 30-second discharge pulse at a rate of 1C. The computed resistance shows that, for a given SOC, cell degradation resulted in a significant increase in resistance compared to the fresh cell, but approached a similar performance at a driven distance of 150 k to 250 k kilometers. The resistance peaks observed at 0.7 SOC for the 0 k and 100 k km cells shifted toward higher SOC regions in the 150 k and 250 k km cells. As the aged cells lost capacity with increasing distance, their electrochemical responses shifted across different SOC [18–20], as discussed further in Fig. 3 .

To further diagnose the full-cells and determine the modes of degradation prior to cell teardown for post-mortem analysis, an additional test was performed involving a low current discharge at C/20. Utilizing the voltage and discharge capacity calculated from the test, differential capacity analysis (DCA), also known as incremental capacity analysis (ICA), was performed. The DCA involves calculating the derivatives of voltage and capacity for the complete discharge profile and comparing the ratio of differential capacity to voltage (dQ/dV) with respect to the cell terminal voltage during discharge. Both cathode and graphite anode experience a series of phase changes and the combined effect of these phase changes can be observed as peaks in the DCA plot as shown in Fig. 3. When comparing the differential capacity analysis (DCA) plots of a fresh cell at the beginning of its life to aged samples with varying levels of degradation, changes in the peaks are observed. These changes include shifts in the peak locations relative to voltage and variations in the peak magnitude. In our previous work, the changes in the DCA profiles during cell aging can be related to specific degradation mechanisms occurring at one or both electrodes [21–23]. In this work, the team was able to perform DCA on data for the samples of 100 k km and 200 k km and based on the changes observed in the peaks as shown in Fig. 3, diagnose the presence of both Li loss and active material loss.

From the data, the presence of Li loss shifts dQ/dV peaks toward higher voltage due to the change in the utilization window of the anode OCP [21,22]. The reduction in the peak magnitude can be attributed to the active material loss as a degradation mechanism of electrodes. In both the 100 k and 200 k km aged cells, the DCA peaks around 3.8 V shift to higher voltages, while the peaks around 3.6 V significantly reduce in magnitude. This result suggests that both Li loss and active material loss are the main causes of capacity loss in the full-cells. In order to quantify and verify each degradation mechanism, cell teardown and electrochemical evaluation of individual electrodes were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of cycle-aged graphite anodes
ce:section.ce:para: To avoid any potential damages to the graphite SEI by exposure to ambient air, coin half-cells were fabricated in Ar-filled glovebox. Due to the reduced sampling area of the coin cells (1.33 cm2), the obtained characterization data may present higher degree of uncertainty. This inherent ambiguity was addressed by fabricating multiple coin cells to enhance the overall data reliability. Eight coin cells were fabricated for both the 100 k km and 250 k km aged anodes to ensure statistical accuracy in confirming their capacities. The resulting data is depicted in Fig. 4 and tabulated in Table 1 as “Residual capacity of anodes”. There is some degree of variance between individual cells, but upon averaging the data, 100 k km aged anode is shown to retain a capacity of 4.32 mAh, while the 250 k km aged anode exhibits a capacity of 4.25 mAh. This capacity comparison indicates that the 100 k km aged cell experiences a capacity loss of 0.08 mAh compared to its fresh counterpart, whereas the 250 k km aged cell encounters a capacity loss of 0.15 mAh. It was reported that the prolonged cycling leads to a fracture of active material, electrically disconnecting it from the main body of electrode [24,25]. It is attributed to a 12 % volume change of graphite during cycling, which can induce damage within the anode.

Similarly, the residual capacity of aged cathodes was evaluated using pouch-type half-cells with an approximate area of 24 cm2 to assess their performance (see Fig. 5 ). The results are summarized in Table 1 under “ Residual capacity of cathodes.” The residual capacities of both cathodes and anodes decrease with increasing driven distance. The data from half-cell characterizations of both the cathode and anode, presented in the subsequent section, were scaled to the 40 Ah pouch cells. For any given distance, the N/P ratios based on residual capacities consistently exceed 1, even after accounting for capacity degradation. This result suggests that the residual capacity of aged cathodes is limiting the overall capacity of the full-cell.

Aged cathode half-cell voltage profiles shown in Fig. 5(a) encompass an Li compensation step and a full charge/discharge cycle. The Li compensation step (refer to as Step 1) here means supplying aged cathode with Li+ ions so to have it be back to its original state. This amount of Li ion supplied back to aged cathode represents the Li loss to the irreversible side reactions, such as formation and repair of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) during cell operation. Following this logic, a discernible trend surfaces in relation to driving distance and Li loss amount. Fig. 5(b) shows Li loss values ranging from 4.32 Ah for the fresh (0 k km) cell to 6.95 Ah for the 100 k km aged cell, 9.66 Ah for the 150 k km aged cell, and 8.80 Ah for the 250 k km aged cell. Interestingly, a minor reduction in Li loss is observed when transitioning from 150 k km to 250 k km aging, this observation aligns with the increased capacity exhibited by the 250 k km full-cell data measured at C/3. Specifically, the 250 k km cell demonstrates a capacity of 35.91 Ah, surpassing the 35.37 Ah capacity of the 150 k km cell.

In addition to Li loss, Fig. 5(c) also compares the first cycle capacity of the cathode. Analogous to the Li loss case, a clear trend is evident, indicating that higher driving distances result in a greater decrease in achievable capacity. The difference between achievable capacity in a half-cell setting indicates that there is less active material that accommodate Li ions reversibly during cycling process. In other words, this decrease in cathode capacity can be attributed to active material loss, wherein some portion of active material is disconnected from bulk and being electrochemically inactive. This phenomenon can be caused by delamination of electrode or particle pulverization during full-cell operation, leading to a corresponding decrease in capacity. However, upon inspection, we found no evidence of cathode delamination in any cells, indicating that the capacity loss may stem from the particle isolation. It should be noted that active material loss is considerably lower compared to Li loss, with the 250 k km aged cell exhibiting the largest active material loss equal to 1.47 Ah, roughly ∼13 % of the total loss.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of overall degradation mechanisms in EV cells
ce:section.ce:para: Having identified that cathode is the pivotal factor governing full-cell capacity, subsequent analyses concentrate primarily on cathode half-cell characterizations, with the objective of dissecting the underlying capacity loss mechanisms linked to the aged cathodes. A bar plot illustrated in Fig. 6(a) offers an insightful breakdown of capacity loss contributions for various aging conditions of EV cells with various driven distances, respectively 0, 50 k, 100 k, 150 k, and 250 k km. These mechanisms are classified into two categories, Li loss prompted by SEI formation and active material loss stemming from active material loss. Evident from the trend, greater driving distances are correlated with higher total capacity losses. From the breakdown shown in the bar plot, Li loss constitutes the majority of capacity loss, followed by a smaller fraction of material loss.

The fresh cell (0 km distance) cathode already display a Li loss equivalent to 4.32 Ah of capacity despite it have not been used for EVs. This Li-loss occurred during the formation cycle of the full-cells in manufacturing process. Considering this, the actual capacity fading attributable to Li loss for each driving distance, after deducting the Li loss from the formation cycle, has been calculated as 2.31 Ah for 50 k km, 2.63 Ah for 100 k km, 5.34 Ah for 150 k km, and 4.48 Ah for 250 k km, respectively. The cell with 150 k km has larger amount of Li loss compared to the cell with 250 k km, which can be attributed to the difference aging history of EV or manufacturing date, which is part of the uncertainty in real work EVs.

At lower vehicle driven distances, such as 50 k km, approximately 100 % of capacity loss is attributed to Li loss due to SEI formation. However, as the driven distance increases (> 100 k km), there is a steady rise in capacity fading resulting from the active material loss. This trend can be explained by the fact that the stress from the volume change of active materials during cycling is not yet severe enough to induce mechanical degradation in the cathode's active materials.

In Fig. 6(b), a comparative analysis is presented for the residual capacity derived from the full-cell tested at C/20 rate (Fig. 2a) and estimations based on the cumulative capacity loss calculated from half-cell experimental data. The calculated residual capacities from the full-cell tests stand at 100 % for 0 km, 93.2 % for 50 k km, 90.4 % for 100 k km, 85.9 % for 150 k km, and 85.8 % for 250 k km distances, respectively. Notably, these full-cell data (green square) exhibit a good agreement with the total capacity fading values (yellow triangle) from each degradation source (active material loss and Li loss) which was determined by the analysis in previous sections. As a result, the residual capacity of EV cells, relative to driven distance, can now be accurately correlated with specific sources of degradation. The observation of a linear increase in active material loss indicates that mechanical degradation progresses linearly with vehicle mileage beyond 50 k km. As discussed earlier, Li loss emerges as the major source of degradations until reaching to around 150 k km. Beyond 150 k km, the steady increase in active material loss becomes the main factor in continuous capacity decaying.

The capacity loss caused by cell impedance growth was assessed by comparing capacity recorded at C/20 vs. C/3 rates. However, the results showed negligible capacity losses from the cell impedance growth, suggesting that the commercial EV cells were effectively managed to suppress impedance growth, undetectable by these testing protocols. Nonetheless, overall cell resistance growth with driving mileage was observed in Fig. 2(b), which affects the power performance of lithium-ion batteries. Therefore, we further characterized the impedance growth behaviors in the cathode and anode using EIS and DRT techniques, discussed in the following section.

Under our EV cell testing protocols, which involve CC-CV charging followed by CC discharging, the impact of the C-rate during discharging was found to be minimal across the range from C/20 to C/3-rate, with the capacity variance remaining within 1 %. However, as inferred by data shown in Fig. 2(b), the resistance of actual EV cell can govern its high pulse power capability, which is crucial for vehicle's real-world operation. Therefore, we performed systematic EIS and DRT characterizations on the EV cells depending on mileage, by employing cathode/cathode and anode/anode symmetric cell configurations. These symmetric cells offer the significant advantage of eliminating artifacts, such as a large SEI impedance from Li-metal in half-cells, thereby enabling the collection of highly accurate impedance data for individual electrodes [11,26]. As shown in Fig. 2(b), all aged cells exhibited a distinct trend of increasing resistance with higher mileage at 50 % SOC. Therefore, three representative cells with 50 k, 100 k, 250 k km mileage were selected at 50 % SOC for EIS and DRT analyses.

Fig. 7 illustrate the Nyquist plots of cathode and anode symmetric cells with different mileages. The SOC of all the pairs of electrodes was set to 50 %. In overall, both cathode and anode symmetric cells revealed a consistent trend of increased impedance with driven distance. The cathode symmetrical cell data suggests that high-frequency impedance is largely unaffected by distance, while low-frequency impedance shows notable growth with increasing distance. The EIS data was further processed using DRT technique to perform a more detailed analysis [11,27,28]. Fig. 7(b) reveals three distinct DRT peaks, labeled as C1, C2, and C3, arising from the cathode symmetric cells. The C1 at around 105 Hz stems from uncompensated resistance and incomplete Nyquist data in the high-frequency range. The small, unindexed peaks observed at 500 kHz – 1 kHz is attributed to contact impedances including cathode/Al current-collector. The C2 peak appearing at 1 kHz–100 Hz is associated with the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) layer [27]. The final peak, C3, located at 101.5–1 Hz is related to the charge transfer impedance of cathodes [27,29].

The contact and cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) impedance remained consistent, showing no significant changes across varying distances. This result indicates a relatively stable mechanical property of cathode. Despite the observed loss of active material from the cathode, as seen in Fig. 6, the isolated particles are electrically disconnected from the cathode, making them undetectable by EIS and DRT analyses. However, a noticeable growth of charge transfer resistance (C3) was observed, which shifted toward the lower frequency region as driven distance increased. This trend implies that the chemical degradation of the cathode over an extended period progressively aged the surface and impeded the charge-transfer process.

Fig. 7(c) and (d), respectively, shows Nyquist plots and DRT profiles of anode symmetric cells, which changes over distance. Similar to that of cathodes, the graphite anodes show a significant growth in impedance at low-frequency region with increasing distance. In their DRT profiles, as shown in Fig. 7(d), four distinct DRT peaks (A1–A4) are observed. The A1 peak at >0.1 MHz correspond to the uncompensated resistance and incomplete Nyquist data in the high-frequency range. The A2 peak appearing between 104 and 105 Hz, is associated with contact impedance of anodes including particle-particle and/or anode/Cu current-collector [11,27]. The broad A3 peak appearing between 102 and 104 Hz is attributed to SEI layer impedance on graphite anodes. The A4 peak, located at 101–102 Hz, corresponds to charge transfer impedance [27].

A significant increase in A2 peak was observed between 100 k and 250 k km distances intervals. During post-mortem characterization, which involved opening the cells, we found a noticeably weak binding of graphite layer to the Cu current collector. This is caused by the severe growth of SEI (which accompanies the Li loss), resulting in strong adhesion of the graphite layer onto the separator, simultaneously weakening its bond to the Cu foil. As a consequence, the cell at 250 k km exhibited a relatively large area where graphite is bound to the separator and delaminated from the Cu foil, which can explain the substantial contact resistance (A2) in the 250 k km cell. Accompanies with the contact degradation, the 250 k km cell experienced a noticeable increase in the charge-transfer resistance (A4) between 100 k and 250 k km distanceintervals. In addition, the SEI impedance remains relatively stable from 50 k to 100 k km, with a minor increase observed from 100 k to 250 k km. This result is consistent with the Li loss data (Fig. 6), where 50 k and 100 k km drive distances result in similar Li losses, while a minor increase is observed from 100 k to 250 k km.

Importantly, both anode and cathode symmetric cell data demonstrate minor impedance changes between 50 k and 100 k km, whereas a substantial increase is observed when transitioning from 100 k to 250 k km, particularly in the charge-transfer impedance of the cathode and the contact impedance of the anode. The increase in the anode contact impedance is linked to the regeneration of the SEI layer on graphite particles, a process accompanied by electrolyte reduction and Li ion consumption (i.e., the Li loss mechanism).

In summary, systematic electrochemical characterization techniques have been demonstrated as a useful tool for understanding the degradation mechanisms of Li-ion batteries recovered from EV battery packs. Custom-designed electrochemical methods were developed and applied to gauge the impact of cathode and anode degradations in EV cells. The results suggested that aged graphite anode did not experience major capacity degradation. Given the N/P ratio > 1.0 in the cells, cathode performance was the major limiting factor of the overall cell capacity. Based on the electrochemical analysis, the residual capacity of EV cells, relative to driving distance, was accurately correlated with specific sources of degradation: (i) active material loss and (ii) Li loss mechanisms. The quantification of these degradation sources in EV batteries was successfully completed, showing excellent agreement with the actual full-cell capacity data. Furthermore, symmetric cell impedance analysis also revealed that cathode and anode suffered from different degradation mechanisms: the charge transfer resistance for the cathode and contact impedances for the anode. Overall, this work demonstrates effective experimental strategies to reveal Li-ion battery degradation mechanisms in EVs, which can contribute to the continued development of future EV technology.

Jun Wei Yap: Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Michael Lee: Methodology, Investigation. Faissal El Idrissi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Prashanth Ramesh: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dal Young Yoon: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Chun Yong Kang: Supervision, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Marcello Canova: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hanna Cho: Supervision. Jung-Hyun Kim: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially sponsored by Hyundai Motor Company.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Federal Energy Management Program Report Template, (n.d.).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysing the usage and evidencing the importance of fast chargers for the adoption of battery electric vehicles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Neaimeh, S.D. Salisbury, G.A. Hill, P.T. Blythe, D.R. Scoffield, J.E. Francfort, Analysing the usage and evidencing the importance of fast chargers for the adoption of battery electric vehicles, Energy Policy 108 (2017) 474–486. doi:10.1016/j.enpol.2017.06.033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on lithium-ion battery ageing mechanisms and estimations for automotive applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Barré, B. Deguilhem, S. Grolleau, M. Gérard, F. Suard, D. Riu, A review on lithium-ion battery ageing mechanisms and estimations for automotive applications, Journal of Power Sources 241 (2013) 680–689. doi:10.1016/j.jpowsour.2013.05.040.
